content: The most common mental block beginners face when configuring cross-origin resource sharing in an Axum server is the belief that setting allowed origins on their CorsLayer will magically grant all browsers permission to make requests from anywhere. This happens because they often configure the layer once at startup and then assume it applies globally without understanding how the tower middleware actually intercepts and terminates HTTP flows before a request ever reaches your application logic. When you create a single CorsLayer with allowed origins, that configuration is indeed applied to every incoming request regardless of whether it originates from your own domain or not, meaning if you accidentally include a wildcard or an incorrect origin in your list, you might think your CORS is broken when actually the browser simply refuses the request because it cannot match any allowed origin. The real problem emerges when dealing with credentials like cookies or authorization headers; many developers forget that for these to work, the server must explicitly allow them on both preflight and actual requests through the WithCredentials flag in the CorsLayer configuration. Without this flag set correctly, you might see 403 Forbidden responses from your own domain because the browser blocks the request thinking it needs a different origin check or because the credentials were stripped before reaching your handler. The correct approach requires building a CorsLayer using the tower_http crate that explicitly lists every permitted domain as a string slice and then attaches this layer to your router via use_axum_cors with the WithCredentials flag enabled if you need authenticated requests across origins. When handling preflight OPTIONS requests, Axum automatically invokes your handler function but only if the CORS headers match what was configured in the CorsLayer; if you do not set up an explicit OPTIONS route or rely on automatic handling without proper configuration, the browser will still send an OPTIONS request expecting a specific Access-Control-Allow-Origin header that matches the requesting domain, and if that header is missing or wrong, the browser aborts the actual request entirely. The key insight is that CORS headers are not just metadata but strict contractual agreements between client and server enforced by the browser at runtime, so any mismatch in allowed origins or credential flags will cause your application to appear non-functional even though your code might be correct from a Rust perspective. Beginners often also forget that the CorsLayer must be created once per environment and reused consistently across all your routes; mixing different layers for different endpoints leads to inconsistent behavior where some pages work with credentials while others do not, causing confusing 403 errors during development. Finally, when testing locally, remember that localhost is a valid origin but browsers may still block requests unless you explicitly add it or use the wildcard for development only, and never deploy wildcards in production because they expose your API to every untrusted domain on the internet.
